story:
A bag of popcorn kernels, three ripe tomatoes, three wire hangers, and six marbles—that’s what it will take for me to see without glasses. I’m eleven, super nearsighted, stuck with bright-pink frames I loved at eight and hate now. Dad sends me to the upper field to check what’s beating paths in the hay, and I find it: a caterpillar-ish thing a little bigger than me, in a velvet vest with sparkly pockets, humming while stuffing its many hands into those pockets and flinging out hay, stones, dirt, even a bottle cap.

I try to act like a big kid and negotiate. It swivels its tendrils toward me, squeaks “Yeeksi day!” and calls me “little boy.” It claims it isn’t eating the hay—just needs it for construction. When I ask where it’s from, it says “near and far,” talks about time being less forward and distance less far, and calls itself a “next-door neighbor of alternate being.” Not exactly alien. Not exactly here. It notices my glasses and wonders if I have magic seeing that crosses barriers; turns out it’s the one stuck between dimensions.

I tell it to leave, and it tries, only to get snagged in burdocks at the field’s edge. I help pick out the burrs (it eats one—“Tasty!”), and it panics about being seen. No one can know it’s been here, not even my dad. Then it makes an offer: if I keep quiet, it’ll fix my eyes. I worry about telemarketer-type promises and, you know, blindness. It asks for popcorn (unpopped), three tomatoes, wire, and—could I produce marbles?

I sprint home and back with a leather bag of kernels, three tomatoes, three coat hangers, and six of my brother’s marbles. The creature starts working. When I ask for the eye-fixing first, it points to a puddle of its own slime and says a little of that will do. I say hard no. Without waiting for a real yes—which is not okay—it slurps some up a tendril and blasts it into my eyes.

It’s disgusting. I scream and scrub at the sticky strings—and then the world locks into focus. I can see the barn roof, silo, and mountains, crisp as anything. My glasses on the ground look like a toy. The creature says it won’t be “permanent” the way things are when you’re eleven—because I’m still changing—but as long as my eyes stay mine, I’ll see like this until I change again. “Sight is a matter of perception,” it says.

After building a tomato hoop, it vanishes. The air inside the hoop shimmers like heat; I hear marbles clinking together, popcorn popping, a cheerful “Delicious!” and a burp. I drag the wire-and-tomato hoop under the burdocks and kick hay over the shiny bits—no one needs to find that. When I put my glasses on, they only blur now. Walking home, I’m giddy—every leaf, every wave of grain, sharp and beautiful.

Then the extra seeing starts. A flicker becomes a pointy red cone; gnomes, pixies, a trollish lump, and a parade of walking mushrooms appear and blink away. They’re not quite in my world; when I reach out, my hand passes through. At home, gremlins crawl out of the fridge, a mouse in a prom dress slides the banister, and fairies nest in my bean bag before zipping through the walls. I can’t hear them, and they mostly ignore me, but they’re everywhere: our next-door neighbors of alternate being, “near and far.”

I keep the creature’s secret—it did keep its promise—but I’m clear that shooting mystery goo into someone’s eyes without consent is not okay, even if it worked. Now I’m learning to live with double vision—my world and the one next door—and keeping watch. If that velvet-vested caterpillar ever tromples our hay again, I’ll march it, politely and immediately, back to its tomato hoop.
